name: browser-automation
description: Enterprise-grade browser automation using WebDriver protocol. Use when the user needs to automate web browsers, perform web scraping, test web applications, fill forms, take screenshots, monitor performance, or execute multi-step browser workflows. Supports Chrome, Firefox, and Edge with connection pooling and health management.
capabilities: Navigate to URLs and manage browser sessions with connection pooling
Fill and submit web forms with automatic element detection
Capture full-page and element screenshots for visual testing
Execute JavaScript and evaluate page performance metrics
Handle multi-step workflows with implicit and explicit waits
Scrape dynamic content from JavaScript-heavy websites
author: EmilLindfors
securitySummary: The static analyzer detected 379 potential issues across documentation and example files. After evaluation, all findings are FALSE_POSITIVES - patterns appear in documentation examples, not executable code. The skill is a legitimate browser automation tool using WebDriver protocol with no malicious intent or exploitable vulnerabilities.
valueStatement: Manual browser testing and web scraping are time-consuming and error-prone. This skill provides enterprise-grade browser automation using WebDriver protocol, enabling reliable form filling, screenshot capture, performance monitoring, and multi-step web workflows across Chrome, Firefox, and Edge.
limitations: Requires separate WebDriver server installation for target browsers
Cannot automate browser extensions or developer tools directly
Limited support for browser-specific features outside WebDriver spec
Performance varies based on browser choice and system resources

useCases.targetUser: QA engineers and test automation developers
useCases.title: Automated end-to-end testing
useCases.description: QA engineers use this skill to create reliable browser tests that verify web application functionality across different browsers without manual intervention.

useCases.title: Web data extraction at scale
useCases.description: Data engineers and researchers automate scraping of dynamic websites that require JavaScript rendering, extracting structured data for analysis pipelines.

useCases.title: Automated form processing
useCases.description: Business users automate repetitive web-based form submissions, data entry tasks, and workflow processes that span multiple web pages.

promptTemplates.title: Basic browser navigation
promptTemplates.prompt: Use the browser automation skill to navigate to https://example.com and tell me what the page title is.

promptTemplates.title: Form submission automation
promptTemplates.prompt: Go to the login page at https://example.com/login, fill in the username field with 'testuser' and password field with 'password123', then click the submit button and report the result.

promptTemplates.title: Screenshot capture
promptTemplates.prompt: Navigate to https://example.com/dashboard, wait for the page to fully load (5 seconds), and take a full-page screenshot saved to /tmp/dashboard.png. Report the file path when complete.

promptTemplates.title: Multi-step workflow automation
promptTemplates.prompt: Perform the following multi-step workflow: 1) Go to https://example.com/shop, 2) Search for 'wireless headphones', 3) Click on the first product, 4) Add it to cart, 5) Navigate to cart page, 6) Take screenshot of cart contents. Report each step result.

bestPractices: Use explicit waits with timeout settings instead of fixed sleep delays for reliable element detection across different page load speeds.
Keep browser drivers updated to match browser versions for compatibility and security patches.
Store screenshots and downloads in designated directories with proper cleanup to avoid disk space issues during long-running automations.
antiPatterns: Avoid hardcoding selectors without fallback strategies - page structure changes will break your automations.
Do not skip error handling for network timeouts and element not found exceptions - these are common in real-world web automation.
Avoid running multiple browser instances without connection pooling in production environments to prevent resource exhaustion.

faq.question: What browsers are supported by this skill?
faq.answer: This skill supports Chrome, Firefox, and Edge browsers through WebDriver protocol. Each browser requires a compatible WebDriver binary installed separately on your system.

faq.question: How do I install WebDriver for my browser?
faq.answer: Download the appropriate WebDriver (ChromeDriver for Chrome, GeckoDriver for Firefox, or EdgeDriver for Edge) from the official vendor websites. Place it in your system PATH or configure the location in skill settings.

faq.question: Can this skill handle CAPTCHA or bot protection?
faq.answer: No, this skill cannot bypass CAPTCHA, reCAPTCHA, or other bot protection mechanisms. These require external services or manual intervention.

faq.question: How do I handle dynamic content that loads asynchronously?
faq.answer: Use explicit waits with element presence or visibility conditions instead of fixed sleep timers. The skill provides wait_for_element functions that poll until the element is ready.

faq.question: What happens if a page element is not found?
faq.answer: The skill will raise an ElementNotFound error by default. Wrap operations in try-catch blocks to handle these gracefully, or use the wait_for_element functions that include timeout handling.

faq.question: Can I run this skill in headless mode?
faq.answer: Yes, configure the browser initialization with headless=true option for server environments or CI/CD pipelines where graphical display is not available.
